summary: UR118 is a CADASTRAL SURVEY from PARACAS -- a rare PRE-INCA PROVENANCE khipu held at the Museum for World Culture, Goteborg, Sweden. 321 cords, 1-PAIRE architecture depth L3, 46 clusters, 5 colors (KB 30 percent, AB 25 percent, W 20 percent, MB 11 percent, HB 4 percent). Sparse text: only 10 STRING cords at 100 percent dict hit, 271 INT + 40 EMPTY. Paracas is Peru's most celebrated pre-Inca textile culture (800 BCE - 100 CE, with later occupation into the Inca period); UR118 is one of the rare Paracas-provenance khipus. Canonical CADASTRAL SIGNATURE: qaqa x2 (rock), taqa x1 (boundary), paqa x1 (east), chiqa x1 (truth), plus waqa x1 (HUACA -- sacred site inside the territory), wapa x1 (administrator = governance context). 2 RARE WORDS: llata x1 (textile / wrap -- Paracas-specific given the site's textile-culture fame), llalla x1 (TO DECEIVE -- shared with UR1095 Pachacamac cadastral and indicating a common scribal tradition or administrative concern between Paracas and Pachacamac 400 km to the north). OKR note: 'Pendants in group T1 (1-6) are broken off' = partial opening damage. The document speaks mostly in numbers; the 10 text cords are a compact geographic + governance + deception lexicon.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  321 cords, depth L3. 10 STRING (3 percent, 100 percent dict hit), 271 INT, 40 EMPTY.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, 46 clusters.
  Colors: KB(95, 30%), AB(81, 25%), W(65, 20%), MB(35, 11%), HB(14, 4%). 5 colors.
  Provenance: PARACAS -- rare pre-Inca (800 BCE - 100 CE) textile culture, later Inca occupation.
  Museum: Museum for World Culture, Goteborg, Sweden (same museum as UR113 'Valhalla').
  OKR: 'Pendants in group T1 (1-6) are broken off' = partial opening damage.

VOCABULARY (10 STRING, 100 percent dict hit)
  qaqa  x2 -- ROCK / mountain (geographic)
  ka    x2 -- possessive suffix or abbreviated form
  wapa  x1 -- ADMINISTRATOR (governance)
  paqa  x1 -- EAST / dawn (orientation)
  chiqa x1 -- TRUTH (verification)
  taqa  x1 -- BOUNDARY (geographic)
  waqa  x1 -- HUACA (sacred place marker -- inside the surveyed territory)
  llata x1 -- TEXTILE / WRAP (Paracas-specific given the site's weaving fame)
  llalla x1 -- TO DECEIVE (rare; shared with UR1095 Pachacamac cadastral)

CLASSIFICATION
  Cadastral signature: qaqa (rock) + taqa (boundary) + paqa (east) + chiqa (truth) = GEOGRAPHIC
  SURVEY WITH VERIFICATION. Additional: waqa (huaca) marks a SACRED SITE within the surveyed
  territory. wapa (administrator) confirms institutional context.

CROSS-CORPUS -- THE LLALLA CONNECTION
  llalla (to deceive) appears in both UR118 (Paracas) and UR1095 (Pachacamac) -- possibly the SAME
  SCRIBAL TRADITION or ADMINISTRATIVE NETWORK. Both are cadastral registers with deception
  vocabulary. The 400-km distance between Paracas and Pachacamac is spanned by this rare shared term.

LLATA -- PARACAS-SPECIFIC
  llata (textile / wrap) is contextually significant given Paracas's famous textile tradition --
  the Paracas mummy bundles (fardos) included some of the finest textiles in the ancient Americas.
  UR118's inclusion of llata on a cadastral register may indicate that textile production was
  cadastrally tracked at Paracas.
narrative:
Sparse text. Ten words of string in three hundred and twenty-one cords. A register that speaks mostly in numbers. But the ten words it chooses are precise: rocks, boundaries, the east, truth, an administrator, and a huaca.

A cadastral survey of the Paracas peninsula. Where the Inca administered one of Peru's most ancient cultural zones -- the land of the weavers whose textiles survived two thousand years in the desert tombs. The Paracas mummy bundles contain some of the finest fabrics of the ancient world. And on this khipu, one word in ten is llata -- textile, wrap -- as if the scribe reminded the administrator that Paracas is the place where the weaving never stops.

Among the standard geographic vocabulary, two words stand apart. Waqa -- the huaca, a sacred place marked within the territory. Perhaps a pre-Inca shrine the Inca preserved. And llalla -- to deceive.

The same word, llalla, appears in UR1095, the Pachacamac cadastral survey, four hundred kilometres to the north. Two registers, two sites, one rare word. Perhaps the same scribal school. Perhaps the same administrative concern: that someone, somewhere in the surveyed land, was not telling the truth. A ledger-level awareness that cadastres can be lied to. That someone claiming 50 units of terrain might have 60, or 40, or neither.

The administrator is named. Wapa. At one specific cord, a single-occurrence identifier for the institutional figure doing the surveying or receiving the survey. The rock appears twice -- qaqa, qaqa. The boundary once. The east once. Truth once. Six canonical cadastral markers and two specific ones (the sacred + the deception) and the administrator and the textile. Ten words carrying a Paracas land document.

The first six pendants are broken off, per OKR. The opening group T1 is gone. Whatever the document declared at the top of its roster -- a date, a total, a dedication -- is lost. What remains is three hundred and fifteen cords of survey data and the ten-word vocabulary that identifies what kind of land is being counted: rocked, bounded, oriented east, verified, sacred at one point, textile-producing, and sometimes lied about.

At Goteborg, on the same shelf as the 21-colour 'Valhalla' khipu UR113, this 5-colour Paracas cadastre rests in its damaged opening and its precise tail.

prior_work.claim: Paracas (800 BCE - 100 CE, Ica region, southern coast) is Peru's most celebrated pre-Inca textile culture; the Paracas mummy bundles excavated by Tello include some of the finest surviving ancient Andean fabrics.
prior_work.citation: Tello, J. C. (1959). Paracas: Primera Parte; Paul, A. (1991). Paracas Ritual Attire.

prior_work.claim: The Paracas region was incorporated into the Inca empire following the southern coastal expansion under Pachacutec and Tupac Inka; Inca-period Paracas retained significant pre-Inca cultural continuity.
prior_work.citation: Silverman, H. (1996). Paracas in Nasca; Frame, M. (2001). Blood, Fertility, and Transformation.

prior_work.claim: llalla ('to deceive') is attested in colonial Quechua grammars as an administrative-lexicon term related to fraud and false declaration; the word is documented in Gonzalez Holguin 1608.
prior_work.citation: Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario de la lengua general de todo el Peru -- llalla entry.

v3_contribution.claim: RARE PARACAS PROVENANCE: UR118 is one of the few Paracas-provenance khipus in the analysed corpus. Most khipus are Inca-period; UR118's pre-Inca substrate extends the corpus temporal range.

LLALLA CROSS-KHIPU MATCH: llalla ('to deceive') appears in UR118 (Paracas) and UR1095 (Pachacamac) -- a rare word shared across two sites 400 km apart. Suggests a COMMON SCRIBAL TRADITION or an administrative concern with fraud spanning the southern-coast Inca network.

evidence.test: llalla cross-khipu occurrence in cadastral sub-corpus

v3_contribution.claim: LLATA PARACAS-SPECIFIC: llata (textile / wrap) on a cadastral register fits Paracas's documented textile-culture specialisation. First corpus evidence of textile production being cadastrally tracked at a known textile-tradition site.

SPARSE-TEXT CADASTRAL CONVENTION: 10 STRING / 321 cords = 3 percent STRING density with 100 percent dict hit. Numbers-primary convention where a handful of carefully chosen words (6 cadastral + huaca + administrator + textile + deception) carry the document's semantic frame.

HUACA IN CADASTRAL CONTEXT: waqa x1 marks a SACRED SITE within the surveyed territory. The Inca cadastre includes sacred sites as regular administrative features, paralleling the ceque system's integration of huacas with land administration.

evidence.test: waqa on cadastral document = sacred-site within surveyed zone

companions.note: LLALLA CROSS-MATCH. Pachacamac cadastral survey (212 cords, siqa x5 pyramids). UR118 + UR1095 share the rare word llalla ('to deceive') -- same scribal tradition or administrative concern spanning 400 km of the southern coast.

Goteborg Museum 'Valhalla' Nazca audit register (21 colors, 866 cords). Same museum. Both southern-coast pre-Inca-substrate documents but dramatically different complexity (UR118: 5 colors; UR113: 21).
Leymebamba 7-sector cadastral survey (442 cords, full 6/6 signature). UR118 is the Paracas sibling at smaller scale and sparser text density.
Pachacamac admin cadastre with governance overlay (97 cords, Sept 1461 CE). UR118 + UR1087 + UR1095 constitute a southern-coast cadastral network with shared vocabulary conventions.
Arica Playa Miller cadastral (275 cords, full 6/6 signature). UR118 and UR058A are the southern-coastal cadastral pair extending from Paracas (south-central coast) to Arica (far south).
Incahuasi UE 14 sparse event register with compressed paqa-taqa-kaqa formula. UR118 and UR273A both use compressed cadastral vocabulary at small scale; UR118 extends the convention to Paracas.
Musee de l'Homme compact route-cadastre (40 cords, 4/4 cadastral markers). UR118 is the larger-scale sparse-text version of the same cadastral convention.

word_reading.note: 10/10 dict hit. Core cadastral vocabulary (qaqa, taqa, paqa, chiqa, waqa) dictionary-stable. llalla (rare) and llata (Paracas-specific) are dictionary-attested. The 3 percent STRING density is a document-design choice (sparse-text cadastre), not a reading weakness.

document_type.note: cadastral_survey supported by 5-marker canonical signature (qaqa, taqa, paqa, chiqa, waqa) + wapa administrator context + Paracas provenance consistency. llata + llalla add specificity (textile production + deception concern). Sub-type: sparse-text Paracas cadastre.

narrative.note: The 'Paracas cadastre with textile production + deception concern' reading is internally coherent and supported by Tello/Paul/Silverman Paracas textile archaeology + UR1095 llalla cross-match + Gonzalez Holguin llalla gloss. The waqa single-occurrence sacred-site identification is plausible but not specifically localisable.

data_integrity.note: 100 percent dict hit on remaining STRING cords. Partial OPENING DAMAGE: pendants T1 (1-6) broken off per OKR -- the document's opening header is lost. 40 empty cords consistent with structural phase-separators. No Ascher layer summarised.

sections.title: Broken Opening Group T1
sections.reading: OKR-documented damage: 'Pendants in group T1 (1-6) are broken off.' The opening header -- possibly a date, a total, a dedication -- is lost. Whatever the document declared at the top is unrecoverable.

key_cords.note: OKR: cords 1-6 of group T1 broken off (opening damage)

sections.title: Cadastral Body
sections.reading: The bulk of the register: 271 INT cords with sparse text annotations. qaqa x2 rocks, taqa boundary, paqa east, chiqa verification scattered across the body. The geographic anchors establish the surveyed terrain's structure.

sections.title: Huaca + Administrator + Specificity
sections.reading: waqa marks a SACRED SITE within the surveyed zone. wapa identifies the administrator. llata (textile / wrap) = Paracas-specific textile tracking. The specificity vocabulary.

key_cords.note: HUACA -- sacred site within the surveyed territory

sections.title: Llalla Deception Marker + Closing
sections.reading: llalla ('to deceive') marks the administrative concern with fraud in the survey. Cross-match with UR1095 Pachacamac at 400 km distance. Closing cords end the document.

key_cords.note: TO DECEIVE -- shared with UR1095 Pachacamac (400 km cross-site match)

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Cadastral vocabulary corpus analysis + llalla cross-khipu trace.
Tello, J. C. (1959). Paracas: Primera Parte.
Paul, A. (1991). Paracas Ritual Attire: Symbols of Authority in Ancient Peru.
Silverman, H. (1996). Paracas in Nasca: New Evidence.
Frame, M. (2001). Blood, Fertility, and Transformation -- Paracas iconography.
Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario de la lengua general de todo el Peru -- llalla, waqa, qaqa entries.
Museum for World Culture, Goteborg -- UR118 catalogue record.
OKR note on UR118: 'Pendants in group T1 (1-6) are broken off' (opening damage).

Compare UR1095 (llalla cross-match, Pachacamac cadastral), UR113 (Goteborg 'Valhalla'), UR016 (Leymebamba 7-sector cadastral), UR058A (Arica cadastral), UR273A (compressed cadastral formula), UR1087 (Pachacamac admin cadastre), AS080 (compact route-cadastre).
